resources.string: Users who contributed to development of ReAppzuku:
RAM usage widget. Tap to kill background apps.
Shizuku/Root shell commands will be used to uninstall this app.\n\nNote: System apps may not be removable on all devices.
Restricting background activity for a system app may cause issues with notifications, widgets, VPN, keyboard, accessibility features, or device instability.\n\nProceed at your own risk.
%1$s is in ReAppzuku\'s restriction list, but Android isn\'t restricting it.\n\nResume restriction or remove from list?
%1$s is restricted by Android, but not in ReAppzuku\'s app list.\n\nAdd to ReAppzuku or lift restriction?
Choose required restrictions for each app manually.
Whitelist mode is selected, but whitelist is empty. In Root mode this will kill all system processes, causing a reboot (Kernel/System panic). Add essential apps to whitelist first.
Failed to get running apps output
Some restrictions could not be applied
No Shizuku access — app can\'t continue.
Required for all app features to work correctly.
Run Auto-Kill when RAM usage exceeds

On some ROMs, am force-stop may be interpreted by the system as a signal to reclaim RAM immediately — forcing all apps to close on every Auto-Kill run, regardless of settings.\n\nIf you hit this issue, switch to am kill. It\'s a softer command, less aggressive toward the system.
Restrict background activity for selected apps
Apps to freeze in sleep mode
Idle time before apps are frozen
ReAppzuku needs to restart for this to work. App will be force-closed now.
Free RAM by clearing app caches
Select apps to hide from main list
Schedule time windows to protect apps
No apps with active restrictions or sleep mode
Select at least one restriction type
Start and end time must differ
System apps are critical for device stability. Blocking or stopping them (e.g. Security on Xiaomi) may cause crashes, boot loops, or malfunction.\n\nOnly do this if you know what you\'re doing.

You've selected %1$d system app(s) for background restriction.

This may break notifications, widgets, VPN, keyboards, accessibility, device pairing, or cause instability.

Continue?
Apps with background restrictions may stop syncing, miss notifications, stop updating widgets, or fail background tasks until opened again.\n\nContinue?

Limits background activity while keeping basic functionality.
• Prevents standard background services from starting.
• App can still show important notifications.
• Good for messengers and email.

Moves the app to the Rare standby bucket and blocks key background operations: launching services, scheduling jobs, and triggering alarms. The app works normally while on screen, but freezes as soon as you leave it.

Maximum isolation. App goes fully “asleep”.
• Blocks even Foreground services.
• Blocks Wake-locks.
• Disables autostart on reboot.
• Removes app from battery optimization whitelist.
No saved background restrictions to re-apply.
Saved settings restored. Grant Shizuku/Root access to re-apply background restrictions.
Saved in ReAppzuku, but not applied
Last 12 hours: %1$d apps | Auto-Kills %2$d | Relaunches %3$d | RAM freed %4$s
Apps with the highest Auto-Kill or relaunch count
Sort by Auto-Kill count, relaunches, RAM freed.
No background restriction events yet.\n\nEntries are stored in app database and trimmed automatically.
Not enough data yet.\nSnapshots collected every 30–60 min.\nCheck back later.
No data collected yet. Please wait ~30 mins.
Not enough history yet — check back later.
Snapshots are too close. Try again later.
⚠ Incomplete data: history is still accumulating
Estimated data from Android batterystats / procstats. Actual values may differ. Battery stats may be inaccurate after long charging sessions.
Stats are only collected while background service is active. Enable it in settings.
App may be in memory but isn\'t actively keeping itself alive.
App launched a background service with a persistent notification. Most reliable way to avoid being killed — Android won\'t touch such processes while notification is visible.
App is subscribed to system events — starts automatically when they fire.
Watches for network changes — wakes up when internet becomes available.
Wake-up alarms (RTC_WAKEUP) pull device out of sleep, even with screen off.
Interval under 1 minute — very aggressive, heavy battery impact.
Interval under 5 minutes — noticeable battery impact.
Regular alarms only fire when device is active — won\'t wake it from sleep.
setExactAndAllowWhileIdle — most aggressive type: fires even in Doze mode.
JobScheduler / WorkManager. App is running %1$d tasks now, %2$d more queued. Typical: sync, downloads, analytics. Android runs them when conditions are right — usually charging or Wi-Fi.
JobScheduler / WorkManager — app is running %1$d background tasks. Typical reasons: sync, downloads, analytics.
JobScheduler / WorkManager — %1$d tasks waiting to run. Android executes them when conditions are right (network, charging). Modern replacement for wake-up alarms.
Added by device manufacturer — always bypasses Doze. Cannot be removed.
App requested Doze exemption ("don\'t optimize battery"). Can run in background anytime. Revoke at: Settings → Battery → Battery optimization.
WakeLock (%1$s) — app asked system to stay awake. Drains battery while held.
Service is declared START_STICKY — Android restarts it after kill. App can\'t be permanently stopped without disabling it.
App exposes a Content Provider — starts automatically when any other app accesses its data (contacts, files, settings, etc.).
App has a Sync Adapter registered with system. Android periodically launches it to sync account data, even when app isn\'t running.
%1$s directly launched this process via service or activity call (callingPackage / clientPackage).
%1$s sent a broadcast with action %2$s, which started this process.
App holds registered PendingIntents. System or other apps can activate them anytime — via notification, AlarmManager, or system event — starting the process.
App starts on every boot via BOOT_COMPLETED. Launches after storage is unlocked.
App starts before lock screen (LOCKED_BOOT_COMPLETED) — very early boot, before PIN/password entry.
App starts twice on boot: right after system starts (LOCKED_BOOT) and again after storage is unlocked (BOOT_COMPLETED).
App is in ACTIVE bucket — no background restrictions applied. If you haven\'t used it recently, something is keeping it active.
WORKING_SET bucket — app is used frequently or system considers it important. Minimal restrictions; jobs and alarms run on schedule.
FREQUENT bucket — app used regularly. Some restrictions on rare background operations.
RARE or RESTRICTED bucket — system has limited background activity. Jobs and alarms are heavily delayed or blocked.
Battery usage since last charge reset.
App has active background network activity. Open TCP connections mean ongoing data exchange with server — typical for messengers, push clients, sync apps.
One or more processes hold a binding to this app\'s service. While held, Android can\'t kill the process.
Google Play Services (GMS) is a common culprit — holds push connections and sync bindings.
Push/FCM binding detected — app is waiting for incoming notifications.
AllowWhileIdle alarms fire even in Doze mode.
App is actively polling hardware sensors. Continuous use drains battery even with screen off.
App is requesting location in foreground. Normal for navigation or fitness apps.
App is requesting location in background. Keeps GPS/network active, drains battery significantly.
Total device wakeups caused by this app since last charge. High numbers indicate aggressive background activity that prevents deep sleep.
Shows broadcasts delivered to app and how many forced a cold start. High launch rate means app is repeatedly killed and restarted.
Process priority: %1$s. Lower adj value = system works harder to keep it alive.
Historical wakelock data from battery stats. App held a wakelock multiple times since last charge.
Foreground service is showing a notification. Channel importance determines how visible and intrusive it is.
Foreground service notification has HIGH or URGENT importance — shows as heads-up banner, very hard for system to suppress. App is nearly impossible to kill automatically.
App holds audio focus and is playing or recording audio. Keeps process alive until focus is released.
App holds transient audio focus (duck/transient) — temporarily suppressing other audio.
App has active MediaSession in PLAYING state. System keeps process alive to handle media controls and notifications.
App has a MediaSession that is paused/stopped but not released. Still keeps process in memory until explicitly abandoned.
App is performing BLE scan. Scanning acquires wake lock internally and keeps process running in background.
App is scanning BLE in LOW_LATENCY mode — most power-intensive scan mode, prevents CPU from sleeping.
App has active GATT connection to peripheral. Connection is maintained by system and keeps process alive.
App registered ContentObservers for content URIs (contacts, media, settings, etc.). Any change to these URIs wakes app to deliver callback.
App is registered for Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM). GMS can wake app anytime a push message arrives.
App is running in multiple OS processes. Sub-processes (e.g. :sync, :remote) can stay alive independently and aren\'t always stopped when main process is killed.
App is registered as active Accessibility Service. System keeps it running at all times while enabled, regardless of battery optimization.
App is currently selected input method (keyboard). System keeps active IME alive permanently while selected.
App is Device Owner or Profile Owner. Can\'t be force-stopped or uninstalled by normal means, runs with elevated permissions.
App is active Device Administrator. Has elevated privileges — protected from force-stop via standard battery restriction mechanisms.
App acquired WakeLock via AppOps — CPU was kept awake on its behalf.
App started a Foreground Service recently — was explicitly brought to active state.
App is permitted to run in background by system battery policy.
App has unrestricted background execution — fully exempted from battery optimization.
App can schedule exact alarms — wakes device at precise times regardless of Doze.
App received user interaction signal recently — may have triggered background launch.
App uses Activity Recognition API — periodically receives motion updates in background.
App was active recently but was not in the foreground — indicates a hidden background wake triggered by an alarm, job, push notification, or chain launch.
Process is frozen by system (Android 11+): execution is paused but not killed. Unfreezes automatically on access.
Android 15: service of type dataSync or mediaProcessing exceeded 6-hour limit. System should have triggered onTimeout() and stopped it.
Android 15: the service has less than 30 minutes remaining out of the 6-hour limit (dataSync/mediaProcessing).
Wakeup source from /sys/kernel/wakeup_sources (fallback for Android 14+ when dumpsys power does not show the wakelock).
FGS blocked when starting from background
Android 12+: attempt to start FGS from background without allowed exemption. Service did not start.
User enabled Data Saver or restricted background network. App can\'t use network in background on mobile data.
Background network access: explicitly allowed (Data Saver exemption)
App was whitelisted in Data Saver settings — has unrestricted network access in background.
Android 11+: app has permission to receive location data from background at any time, even when not in use. Requires separate user approval.
Android 12+: app has permissions for Bluetooth scanning and/or connection. Can initiate scanning on broadcast.
App is holding kernel-level wakelock (/sys/power/wake_lock). Extremely rare — indicates non-standard driver or system component.
System cancelled alarm due to app_standby or battery_saver. App tries to launch via timer but system blocks it.
Android 14+: app received token to launch from background. Usually granted for high-priority FCM, exact alarms, or PendingIntent from visible app.
Android 14+: system blocked background launch attempt. App tried to start Activity or FGS without permission.
Android 13+: dynamically registered receiver with exported=true is accessible to other apps. Can receive broadcasts from any sender.
Android 14+: BOOT_COMPLETED receiver can\'t start FGS of type MICROPHONE (API 34) or PHONE_CALL (API 35). App is trying to bypass this restriction.
Android 15: attempt to start FGS from BOOT_COMPLETED resulted in exception. Service type (dataSync/mediaProcessing/phoneCall) is forbidden from launching at boot.
App was excluded from battery optimization by user or system. Can run in background without Doze/App Standby restrictions.
App is in battery manager exemptions list.
RESTRICTED bucket (Android 12+): Jobs and Alarms are completely blocked. On Android 14, even ALLOW_WHILE_IDLE alarms are ignored. App restricted due to excessive background consumption.
NEVER bucket: background execution entirely disabled by system.
Android 12+: system confirmed RESTRICTED bucket via appops. Jobs and Alarms blocked. App can\'t launch itself independently.
Android 11+: device is in Doze mode (%1$s).
Wakelocks, Network, Jobs, and Alarms (except ALLOW_WHILE_IDLE) are blocked. Apps without Doze exemption can\'t run in background.
Permission to schedule exact alarm triggers. Android 14: denied by default for new installs. If allowed — app can precisely wake device via timer.
Permission to display notifications over lock screen. Android 14+: allowed only for alarm/call apps. Presence in a third-party app is suspicious.
Management of media sessions of other apps. Associated with mediaProcessing FGS type on Android 15.
Permission to run User-Initiated Jobs (long user-initiated tasks). Android 14: replacement for \'long jobs\'. Can run with screen locked.
System blocked launch of Foreground Service. App is trying to operate in background but is restricted.
Android 12+: app can manage media sessions of other apps. On Android 15, linked to mediaProcessing foreground service type.
Android 12+: app holds permission for exact alarm triggers regardless of App Standby bucket. USE_EXACT_ALARM is granted only to system/privileged apps.
Android 12+: app holds permission for exact alarm triggers regardless of App Standby bucket. SCHEDULE_EXACT_ALARM requires explicit user approval.
Auto-Kill runs 10 seconds after the event to allow background apps to start
Run Auto-Kill when target app launches
Detects when target apps are launched to trigger Auto-Kill and free RAM
For the App Launch trigger to work, enable ReAppzuku in Accessibility Settings. The setting has been saved and will activate once the service is enabled.
Schedule up to 2 rule sets by time
Start and end time must differ
This range overlaps with another preset (%1$d:%2$02d – %3$d:%4$02d). Adjust times so presets don\'t overlap.
All settings of preset %d will be reset to defaults. Changes only apply after you tap Save.
Preset %d settings reset — tap Save to apply
You cannot change main Auto-Kill settings when preset is active
